title: The Next Russia Threat
gist: Kofman argues Russia will reconstitute a major military threat to NATO faster than analysts expected after the Ukraine war ends (likely 5-7 years), fielding a larger force heavy in drones and deep-strike capacity, while NATO remains dangerously unprepared for the 'mass precision' battlefield and over-reliant on a wavering United States.

insights.claim: Russia will likely reconstitute its military enough to pose a major threat to NATO within five to seven years after the Ukraine war ends, faster than analysts expected in 2022, and could threaten NATO members or Ukraine in more limited ways shortly after the war closes.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: European defense rearmament has a hard deadline measured in years, not decades; defense-industrial and procurement timelines need to compress now.
insights.quote: Russian military reconstitution after the war is not a question of if but of when.
insights.locator: Opening / 'The war in Ukraine, now in its fifth year'

insights.claim: Russian reconstitution should be measured by regained combat functions and the ability to command and integrate larger formations (armies, corps, divisions) for large-scale combined-arms operations, not merely by restoring prewar troop and equipment numbers.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Threat assessments and the spreads on European defense stocks should weight command/integration capacity, the metric Russia is weakest on, not raw equipment counts.

insights.claim: Despite at least 400,000-500,000 Russian soldiers killed and 600,000-800,000 seriously wounded in Ukraine, Russia's active force has grown from roughly 850,000 prewar to 1.3 million, with many formations doubling or tripling and adding drone, reconnaissance, assault, and electronic-warfare units.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: The 'Russia is bled dry' thesis is wrong; the standing force is larger and more drone-heavy than prewar, a structurally higher European threat baseline.
insights.quote: the size of the force has grown from approximately 850,000 active personnel before the war to 1.3 million now

insights.claim: Russia today likely fields as many or slightly more armored vehicles and tanks than at the war's start, produces over 200 T-90M tanks per year (so half its fleet could be T-90Ms within 7-8 years), and is rapidly producing air-defense systems and missiles.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Replenishment capacity is durable; sanctions on machine tools slow but do not stop the heavy-armor and air-defense pipeline.
insights.locator: QUANTITY OVER QUALITY / 'According to open-source accounts'

insights.claim: Russia makes millions of tactical drones annually and is launching an average of 6,500 large one-way attack drones per month in Ukraine; it built over 70,000 such drones in 2025 and has contracted at least 100,000 more for 2026.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Deep-strike volume against bases, units, and critical infrastructure is the fastest-scaling Russian capability; cheap counter-drone defense is the growth need for NATO and contractors.
insights.quote: in 2025, Russia made more than 70,000 of them, and for 2026 it has contracted to obtain at least 100,000 more

insights.claim: Russia has visibly lost the capacity for large-scale combined-arms maneuver, having lost the battalion and regimental commanders who knew how to run offensives at scale and now fighting with one-to-two-soldier infiltration groups after only ~two weeks of training.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Russia's near-term offensive ceiling is low; the threat window is the reconstitution period, not the immediate post-war moment.
insights.quote: more recently, it has been sending one to two soldiers at a time to infiltrate Ukrainian positions
insights.locator: TWO STEPS FORWARD, ONE STEP BACK / 'Despite improvements in some areas'

insights.claim: The Russian military's enduring weaknesses are cultural and human: overly centralized decision-making, coercion over professionalism, falsified combat reports, endemic corruption (soldiers paying to avoid assaults), and chronic underinvestment in training and NCOs.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: These deficits cap how fully Russia can execute its own sound force designs, the structural reason its rearmament is 'two steps forward, one step back'.
insights.locator: PAST IS NOT PROLOGUE / 'Besides force design'

insights.claim: Gerasimov-era leadership concluded Russia's failures stemmed from not being 'Soviet enough' and dismantled the Joint Strategic Commands meant to integrate branches, leaving the military less integrated and reliant on ad hoc wartime command structures.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Self-inflicted organizational regression is a tailwind for NATO's qualitative edge, but may reverse as combat-experienced officers rise.
insights.locator: RECONSTITUTIONAL CRISIS / 'One reason for the uncertainty'

insights.claim: Russia spends about 40 percent of its budget (eight percent of GDP) on the military, roughly double prewar but below late-Cold-War Soviet levels; the economy has stagnated since 2025 with growing regional deficits and more federal borrowing.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Defense spending is sustainable but increasingly strains the economy; the ceiling on simultaneous inflation control, growth, and military spending is approaching.
insights.quote: Russia is spending 40 percent of its budget, or eight percent of GDP, on the military
insights.locator: SUNK COSTS / 'The Russian economy has been militarized'

insights.claim: In 2026 Russian defense spending is projected at $180 billion in dollar terms but roughly $400-500 billion on a purchasing-power-parity basis because procurement is paid in rubles, which is how Moscow sustains the war and contracts over 400,000 soldiers per year.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Dollar-denominated comparisons badly understate Russian military output; PPP framing reframes the NATO-Russia spending balance.
insights.quote: the effective amount it will spend is about $400 billion to $500 billion

insights.claim: Russia faces a binding labor constraint: a 2.2 percent unemployment rate, skilled-labor shortages, and direct competition between the army and arms factories for the same men, worsened by COVID excess mortality and war casualties.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Demographic and labor scarcity, not money, is the hardest cap on reconstitution speed; watch wage and mobilization signals.
insights.quote: The army needs men to fight. The arms factories need men to supply them.
insights.locator: SUNK COSTS / 'Most obvious, the country lacks enough qualified people'

insights.claim: Russia's ground-force equipment draws heavily on irreplaceable Soviet-era legacy stocks, so as it consumes reserves to outfit the current force it is depleting its future mobilization potential.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: A hidden cap on the size of any future surge force; Soviet inheritance is a wasting asset that new production only partly offsets.
insights.locator: SUNK COSTS / 'Since 2022, Russia has significantly expanded'

insights.claim: NATO militaries do not know how to operate in an environment of 'mass precision', a battlefield where cheap drones outnumber personnel and equipment and conduct real-time reconnaissance and strike, and could suffer much higher losses in the opening days of a future war.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: The capability gap is doctrinal and structural, not just spending; cheap counter-drone and air-defense ecosystems are the urgent procurement priority.
insights.quote: NATO members do not know how to operate in an environment characterized by the presence of mass precision
insights.locator: PAST IS NOT PROLOGUE / 'Consequently, NATO militaries might incur'

insights.claim: NATO's presumed capacity depends on the United States providing forces plus logistics, technical support, and organizational capacity; European militaries evolved to fight alongside the US and are not set up to independently conduct large-scale combat operations.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Reduced US commitment is a first-order risk to European deterrence that more spending alone cannot fix; the gap is in enabling capabilities.
insights.locator: PAST IS NOT PROLOGUE / 'Although it is true that the way Ukraine fights'

insights.claim: With New START expired in February and no replacement, Russia's unconstrained strategic nuclear arsenal combined with its tactical-nuclear advantage, intended to offset NATO conventional superiority, looms over any future contingency.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: The collapse of arms control raises the nuclear-escalation backdrop for any European conflict; a tail risk markets persistently underprice.
insights.locator: PAST IS NOT PROLOGUE / 'Russia also remains a leading nuclear power'

insights.claim: Russia has adapted to blunt Western long-range strike, reorganizing logistics and command so that systems like HIMARS lost much of their shock value, while cheap one-way attack drones now devastate logistics, weapons NATO has not yet acquired at scale.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: The Western precision-strike advantage erodes with exposure; the new edge is cheap attritable drones, where supply chains and volume matter.
insights.locator: TWO STEPS FORWARD, ONE STEP BACK / 'The Russian military has also evolved'

insights.claim: Russia is a power in slow, not sudden, decline; this erosion will hamper but not necessarily prevent reconstitution, and Russia has a history of restoring its military faster than expected after defeat.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Betting on Russian collapse to provide European security is a strategic error; rearmament investment should proceed regardless of war outcome.
insights.quote: Russia has been and remains a power in decline... But that decline is happening slowly, not suddenly.
insights.locator: Closing / 'The Russian military challenge needs to be put in proper perspective'

insights.claim: Analysts repeatedly mis-forecast Russia by over-generalizing from its last war, being slow to see reform after Georgia 2008 then over-correcting after Syria; generalizing from 2026 Ukraine performance to a future NATO-Russia war is appealing but dangerous.
insights.speaker: Michael Kofman
insights.so_what: Discount confident 'Russia is finished' narratives; the historical base rate is that Western analysts under- then over-react.
insights.quote: It is dangerous to generalize from Russia's performance in Ukraine.
insights.locator: PAST IS NOT PROLOGUE / 'But these shortcomings'
